data.title: Mastering Cursor AI: Integrated Programming Workflow
data.summary: A practical, intermediate guide that teaches you how to leverage Cursor AI's advanced models, interaction patterns, and spec‑driven development workflows to build, test, and ship production‑ready applications efficiently.
data.description: A practical, intermediate guide that teaches you how to leverage Cursor AI's advanced models, interaction patterns, and spec‑driven development workflows to build, test, and ship production‑ready applications efficiently.

nodes.title: Chapter 1: Getting Started with Cursor AI
nodes.description: Learn how to install and configure Cursor, select the most suitable AI models, and become familiar with the editor’s core UI elements and shortcuts for a smooth AI‑assisted coding experience.
learning_points: Install and configure Cursor on Windows and macOS
Set up supporting tools (Git, Node.js, Python)
Choose and switch between AI models (OpenAI GPT‑4, Claude, etc.)
Navigate the UI, command palette, AI panel, and keyboard shortcuts

sub_nodes.title: Environment Setup
sub_nodes.description: Step‑by‑step installation of Cursor and required development tools, including environment variables and basic extensions.
learning_points: Download and install Cursor from the official site
Install Git, Node.js, and Python with version managers (nvm, pyenv)
Configure PATH and verify installations with CLI commands

sub_nodes.title: AI Model Selection & Configuration
sub_nodes.description: Understand the available AI backends, API key management, and how to tune model parameters for optimal performance.
learning_points: Compare OpenAI GPT‑4, Claude, and internal models
Add API keys to Cursor settings securely
Adjust temperature, max tokens, and other generation parameters

sub_nodes.title: UI Overview & Keyboard Shortcuts
sub_nodes.description: A tour of Cursor’s interface components, the AI panel, Composer, and essential shortcuts for rapid navigation.
learning_points: Open AI panel (Cmd/Ctrl+L) and Composer (Cmd/Ctrl+I)
Master command palette (Cmd/Ctrl+Shift+P)
Customize keybindings for personal workflow

sub_nodes.title: First AI‑Assisted Code Snippet
sub_nodes.description: Hands‑on exercise to generate, accept, and refine a simple code block using AI suggestions.
learning_points: Write a natural‑language prompt for a basic function
Accept or modify AI‑generated code
Run the function and verify output

nodes.title: Chapter 2: Core AI Interaction Techniques
nodes.description: Deep dive into Rules, Skills, Composer, and prompt engineering to maximize code quality and relevance when working with AI.
learning_points: Create and manage permanent AI instructions using Rules and Skills
Use Composer for multi‑file editing and refactoring
Craft effective prompts and manage conversation context
Practice real‑world scenarios by generating and revising code

sub_nodes.title: Rules & Skills: Permanent AI Instructions
sub_nodes.description: How to write, version‑control, and apply project‑wide instructions that the AI follows in every session.
learning_points: Define coding standards, naming conventions, and security guidelines
Organize Rules files per module or feature
Debug and iterate on instruction quality

sub_nodes.title: Composer for Multi‑File Editing
sub_nodes.description: Leverage Cursor’s Composer to generate or refactor several files in a single request, maintaining consistency across the codebase.
learning_points: Draft composite prompts that specify multiple file targets
Review generated diffs before committing changes
Apply bulk refactors (e.g., renaming, import cleanup)

sub_nodes.title: Prompt Engineering & Context Management
sub_nodes.description: Techniques for structuring prompts, injecting relevant context, and steering AI behavior for precise code generation.
learning_points: Break complex tasks into sequential, incremental prompts
Embed code snippets, comments, and API docs as context
Use explicit constraints to limit scope and enforce style

sub_nodes.title: Hands‑On: Generate a REST API Endpoint
sub_nodes.description: Build a realistic CRUD endpoint, iterate on AI suggestions, and integrate the result into a small Express application.
learning_points: Prompt AI for route handler, validation, and error handling
Accept suggestions, adjust naming, and add middleware
Test the endpoint locally and review AI‑generated tests

nodes.title: Chapter 3: Spec‑Driven Development (SDD) Workflow
nodes.description: Combine Cursor AI with an OpenSpec‑based SDD methodology to go from high‑level requirements to a fully functional application.
learning_points: Write clear, testable specifications using OpenSpec
Scaffold a project structure with AI assistance
Implement core features by following specs and leveraging AI prompts
Deliver a runnable ‘Smart Todo’ application end‑to‑end

sub_nodes.title: OpenSpec & Requirements Engineering
sub_nodes.description: Introduce the OpenSpec format, how to capture functional and non‑functional requirements, and translate them into testable criteria.
learning_points: Define user stories, acceptance criteria, and edge‑case rules
Map spec items to code modules
Create a spec checklist for AI to validate against

sub_nodes.title: Project Scaffolding with AI
sub_nodes.description: Automate folder creation, configuration files, and base classes by prompting AI to generate a consistent project layout.
learning_points: Request AI to produce a file tree with all necessary boilerplate
Include CI/CD configs, linting rules, and environment files
Validate generated scaffolding against the spec

sub_nodes.title: AI‑Assisted Implementation
sub_nodes.description: Iteratively implement business logic, data models, and API routes guided by the spec, using AI to write and refactor code.
learning_points: Translate spec sections into targeted prompts
Use AI to generate domain logic and integrate third‑party libraries
Apply incremental testing and AI‑driven debugging

sub_nodes.title: Capstone Practice: Smart Todo App
sub_nodes.description: End‑to‑end mini‑project: write the spec, scaffold the app, implement features (add, list, filter, persist), and ensure it runs locally.
learning_points: Create OpenSpec document for a Smart Todo app
Prompt AI to generate UI components and backend services
Integrate a database (e.g., SQLite) using AI‑generated migrations
Deploy locally and verify all acceptance criteria

nodes.title: Chapter 4: Advanced Workflow Integration & Production Readiness
nodes.description: Polish the development workflow by integrating automated testing, debugging, version control, and CI/CD pipelines with AI assistance.
learning_points: Automate unit and integration test creation using AI
Diagnose and fix runtime errors with AI‑driven debugging
Manage Git history with AI‑generated commit messages and branch strategies
Optimize AI usage, ensure security, and set up CI/CD for continuous quality

sub_nodes.title: AI‑Generated Automated Tests
sub_nodes.description: Leverage AI to write unit tests for functions, integration tests for API routes, and edge‑case coverage reports.
learning_points: Prompt AI to produce Jest/Mocha or pytest test cases
Generate mock data and edge‑case scenarios
Run tests, interpret coverage output, and iterate

sub_nodes.title: Debugging with AI Assistance
sub_nodes.description: Use AI to parse stack traces, suggest fixes, and guide you through error resolution with contextual context.
learning_points: Paste error logs into AI panel and request root‑cause analysis
Apply AI‑proposed patches and verify behavior
Create a debugging checklist for common runtime issues

sub_nodes.title: Version Control Workflow
sub_nodes.description: Integrate AI into Git processes: write meaningful commit messages, create branch naming conventions, and handle pull requests.
learning_points: Use AI to generate conventional commit messages
Set up branch policies (feature/, hotfix/, etc.)
Draft pull request descriptions and review comments

sub_nodes.title: Performance, Security & CI/CD
sub_nodes.description: Optimize AI token usage, enforce security best practices, and embed AI‑assisted checks into CI pipelines for continuous quality.
learning_points: Implement token budgeting and prompt caching strategies
Scan generated code for common security pitfalls
Configure GitHub Actions or GitLab CI to run AI‑generated tests and lint checks

447.title: Environment Setup
447.description: Step‑by‑step installation of Cursor and required development tools, including environment variables and basic extensions.
key_points: Download and install Cursor from the official site
Install Git, Node.js, and Python with version managers (nvm, pyenv)
Configure PATH and verify installations with CLI commands
447.detailed_content:
## Concept Explanation

Setting up a clean, reproducible programming environment is the first and most critical step when you adopt a new editor like Cursor AI. Cursor relies on the underlying toolchain (Git for version control, Node.js for JavaScript/TypeScript tooling, Python for AI‑powered scripts, and optional runtimes such as Java or Go) to provide features such as real‑time linting, intelligent code completions, integrated terminal sessions, and seamless Git integration. If any of these tools are missing, mis‑configured, or at an incompatible version, you’ll encounter cryptic errors, missing suggestions, or broken shortcuts.

A well‑tuned environment also speeds up day‑to‑day workflows: you can open a project with `cursor .`, run tests from the editor, and have AI‑driven refactors automatically respect the language versions defined in your repository.

## Core Mechanics

1. **Cursor Launch Process** – When you start Cursor, it reads the system `PATH` to locate executables (`git`, `node`, `python`, etc.). It also scans for version‑manager shims (`nvm`, `pyenv`) that dynamically expose the currently‑selected runtime version.
2. **AI Backend** – Cursor’s AI model runs locally (or connects to a cloud service) and invokes language servers (e.g., `typescript-language-server`, `pylsp`) to understand code context. Those servers depend on the exact runtime versions you have installed.
3. **Git Integration** – Cursor uses the `git` CLI to power the source‑control UI. A properly configured `git` with user name/email ensures that commit messages, blame annotations, and branch visualizations work correctly.
4. **Extension Ecosystem** – Cursor’s marketplace hosts extensions (ESLint, Prettier, Black, etc.) that are themselves Node or Python scripts. They must be able to locate the correct interpreter via `PATH`.
5. **Shell Sessions** – The integrated terminal spawns a login shell (bash/zsh) that sources your profile (`~/.bashrc`, `~/.zshrc`). All environment variables set there become available to all tools launched from Cursor.

## Step‑by‑Step Setup

### 1. Download & Install Cursor

- Go to **https://cursor.sh** and download the installer for your OS (Windows, macOS, Linux).
- Run the installer and follow the prompts. On Linux, you may need to make the downloaded AppImage executable:
  
  chmod +x Cursor-linux-x.x.x.AppImage
  ./Cursor-linux-x.x.x.AppImage
  
- Verify the installation by opening a terminal and typing:
  
  cursor --version
  
  You should see the current Cursor version printed.

### 2. Install Git

Most systems already have Git, but if not:

- **macOS** – `brew install git`
- **Debian/Ubuntu** – `sudo apt-get install git`
- **Windows** – Download from **https://git-scm.com** or use `winget install Git.Git`.

Configure your identity (required for Cursor’s Git UI):

git config --global user.name "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "you@example.com"


### 3. Install Node.js with nvm

Using a version manager ensures you can switch between Node versions without permission‑related headaches.

- **macOS/Linux – Install nvm**:
  
  curl -o- https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nvm-sh/nvm/v0.39.0/install.sh | bash
  
- **Windows – Use nvm‑windows**: Download the installer from **https://github.com/coreybutler/nvm-windows/releases**.

After installation, reload your shell and install a recent LTS Node:

source ~/.bashrc   # or source ~/.zshrc
nvm install 18
nvm use 18


Create an `.nvmrc` file in your project (optional) so that `nvm` automatically picks the right version:

echo "18" > .nvmrc


### 4. Install Python with pyenv

- **macOS/Linux – Install pyenv**:
  
  git clone https://github.com/pyenv/pyenv.git ~/.pyenv
  
- **Windows – Use pyenv‑win** or **Conda** (pyenv‑win can be installed via `pip install pyenv-win`).

Add pyenv to your shell startup file:

echo 'export PYENV_ROOT="$HOME/.pyenv"' >> ~/.bashrc
echo 'export PATH="$PYENV_ROOT/bin:$PATH"' >> ~/.bashrc
echo 'eval "$(pyenv init -)"' >> ~/.bashrc
source ~/.bashrc


Install a specific Python version:

pyenv install 3.11.0
pyenv global 3.11.0


Create a `.python-version` file in your project to lock the interpreter:

echo "3.11.0" > .python-version


### 5. Configure PATH and Verify Installations

After adding version managers to your PATH, ensure the order is correct: the shims of `nvm`/`pyenv` should appear before any system‑wide binaries to guarantee the correct runtime is used.

You can check the resolved paths with:

which node   # should point to ~/.nvm/versions/node/v18.x.x/bin/node
which python # should point to ~/.pyenv/shims/python


Run a quick sanity check:

node -v
npm -v
python -V
git --version
cursor --version


All commands should report version numbers without errors.

### 6. Install Essential Extensions (Optional but Recommended)

Open Cursor, press `Ctrl+Shift+X` (or `Cmd+Shift+X` on macOS) to open the Extensions view, and install:

- **ESLint** – For JavaScript/TypeScript linting.
- **Prettier** – Code formatter.
- **Python (Pylance)** – Provides rich language support for Python.
- **GitLens** – Enhances Git integration (requires Git to be on PATH).

Each extension will detect the interpreter you installed via `nvm` or `pyenv` automatically.

## Verifying the Complete Setup

1. **Open a project folder** – Run `cursor .` in a terminal from any repository. Cursor should start with the folder’s files listed in the Explorer.
2. **Run a quick AI request** – Type a comment like `# TODO: Write a function that returns the sum of two numbers` and wait for Cursor’s AI suggestion. The suggestion should be generated without any runtime errors in the status bar.
3. **Check the integrated terminal** – Open a terminal (`Ctrl+``) and execute `node -e "console.log('Hello from Node')"` and `python -c "print('Hello from Python')"`. Both should print their respective messages.
4. **Git operations** – Click the Source Control icon, stage a file, and commit. The commit should succeed, and the author information should match the `git config` you set earlier.

If any of these steps fail, revisit the PATH configuration and ensure the version managers are sourced in the shell that Cursor spawns.

## Optional Tips for Power Users

- **Shell Profiles**: If you use multiple shells (bash, zsh, fish), create a common file (e.g., `~/.profile`) that defines all environment variables and source it from each shell’s startup file. This guarantees consistent behavior regardless of which shell Cursor launches.
- **Docker Integration**: For languages like Go or Rust, you can keep the toolchain inside a container. Cursor can attach to a container via the **Remote - Containers** extension, which still requires the same Git/Node/Python base tools on the host for seamless AI features.
- **Continuous Integration**: Mirror your local environment in CI by installing the same Node and Python versions. Use `.nvmrc` and `.python-version` files as CI inputs (`nvm install` and `pyenv install` respectively).
- **Backup Configuration**: Export your Cursor settings (`cursor --export-settings`) and keep them in a dotfiles repository. This helps you reproduce the environment on a new machine quickly.

## Summary

A proper environment setup is the backbone of a smooth, AI‑augmented development experience with Cursor. By installing Cursor, Git, Node.js via `nvm`, and Python via `pyenv`, and by carefully managing your `PATH`, you ensure that Cursor can locate all required tools, provide accurate code completions, and execute scripts exactly as intended. Verifying each component with simple CLI commands eliminates hidden pitfalls and lets you focus on writing code rather than debugging tooling issues.

## Code Examples

### Example 1

```python
# Install nvm (macOS/Linux) and reload shell
export NVM_DIR="$HOME/.nvm"
curl -o- https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nvm-sh/nvm/v0.39.0/install.sh | bash
source ~/.bashrc

# Install and activate a specific Node version
nvm install 18
nvm use 18

# Create an .nvmrc file for automatic version switching
echo "18" > .nvmrc
```

### Example 2

```python
# Install pyenv (macOS/Linux)
git clone https://github.com/pyenv/pyenv.git ~/.pyenv

# Add pyenv to PATH and initialize
cat >> ~/.bashrc <<'EOF'
export PYENV_ROOT="$HOME/.pyenv"
export PATH="$PYENV_ROOT/bin:$PATH"
eval "$(pyenv init -)"
EOF
source ~/.bashrc

# Install a specific Python version and set global interpreter
pyenv install 3.11.0
pyenv global 3.11.0

# Pin the version in the project
echo "3.11.0" > .python-version
```

### Example 3

```python
# Verify all tools are accessible from PATH
which node   # should output something like ~/.nvm/versions/node/v18.x.x/bin/node
which python # should output something like ~/.pyenv/shims/python
which git    # should output /usr/bin/git (or the installed path)

# Print version numbers for a sanity check
node -v
npm -v
python -V
git --version
```

### Example 4

```python
# Open a project folder with Cursor from the command line
cursor .   # Launches Cursor with the current directory as workspace

# Inside Cursor, open the integrated terminal (Ctrl+`) and run a quick test
node -e "console.log('Node is working')"
python -c "print('Python is working')"
```



## Common Mistakes

- Skipping the `source ~/.bashrc` (or equivalent) after installing nvm or pyenv, which leaves the shims unavailable in the current shell session.
- Installing Node or Python globally without a version manager, leading to version conflicts when different projects require different runtime versions.
- Placing version‑manager PATH entries after system binaries, causing the wrong (often older) system version to be used instead of the managed one.
- Installing Cursor in a directory that contains spaces or special characters (e.g., `C:\Program Files\Cursor`) without quoting paths, which can break CLI shortcuts.
- Forgetting to configure Git user name/email, causing Cursor’s Git UI to show “author unknown” for commits and blame annotations.
- Not verifying the installations with simple `which` and version commands, so misconfigurations remain hidden until an error occurs during development.


## Best Practices

- Always use version managers (nvm, pyenv) and lock runtime versions per project using `.nvmrc` and `.python-version` files, ensuring every teammate and CI job uses identical toolchains.
- Add only the necessary tools to PATH and keep the order correct: user‑local bin directories (nvm shims, pyenv shims) should appear before system directories.
- Create a unified shell profile (e.g., `~/.profile`) that defines all environment variables and source it from all shell init files, guaranteeing consistency across shells opened by Cursor.
- Run a quick sanity check after installation: `which node && node -v && npm -v && which python && python -V && git --version && cursor --version`. This confirms that every required binary is reachable.
- Keep Cursor updated; new releases often include tighter integration with the latest Node/Python versions and improved AI performance.
- Document your environment setup in a README or a dedicated `docs/setup.md` file, including the exact commands you used to install each tool, so others (or future you) can reproduce the environment easily.
code_examples:
# Install nvm (macOS/Linux) and reload shell
export NVM_DIR="$HOME/.nvm"
curl -o- https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nvm-sh/nvm/v0.39.0/install.sh | bash
source ~/.bashrc

# Install and activate a specific Node version
nvm install 18
nvm use 18

# Create an .nvmrc file for automatic version switching
echo "18" > .nvmrc

# Install pyenv (macOS/Linux)
git clone https://github.com/pyenv/pyenv.git ~/.pyenv

# Add pyenv to PATH and initialize
cat >> ~/.bashrc <<'EOF'
export PYENV_ROOT="$HOME/.pyenv"
export PATH="$PYENV_ROOT/bin:$PATH"
eval "$(pyenv init -)"
EOF
source ~/.bashrc

# Install a specific Python version and set global interpreter
pyenv install 3.11.0
pyenv global 3.11.0

# Pin the version in the project
echo "3.11.0" > .python-version

# Verify all tools are accessible from PATH
which node   # should output something like ~/.nvm/versions/node/v18.x.x/bin/node
which python # should output something like ~/.pyenv/shims/python
which git    # should output /usr/bin/git (or the installed path)

# Print version numbers for a sanity check
node -v
npm -v
python -V
git --version

# Open a project folder with Cursor from the command line
cursor .   # Launches Cursor with the current directory as workspace

# Inside Cursor, open the integrated terminal (Ctrl+`) and run a quick test
node -e "console.log('Node is working')"
python -c "print('Python is working')"

448.title: AI Model Selection & Configuration
448.description: Understand the available AI backends, API key management, and how to tune model parameters for optimal performance.
key_points: Compare OpenAI GPT‑4, Claude, and internal models
Add API keys to Cursor settings securely
Adjust temperature, max tokens, and other generation parameters
448.detailed_content:
## Concept Explanation

In Cursor AI, the model you choose determines how code completions, refactorings, and conversational assistance feel and perform. The selection influences latency, cost, privacy, and the kinds of suggestions you receive. Understanding the trade‑offs helps you pick the right backend for each task and avoid unnecessary spend or security risks.

### Why Model Selection Matters

- **Quality vs Speed** – Larger models like GPT‑4 produce more accurate suggestions but have higher latency.
- **Cost** – Each API call consumes tokens; internal models may be cheaper or free.
- **Privacy** – Using an internal model keeps code on‑premises, reducing data‑exposure risk.
- **Domain Strength** – Claude excels at long‑range reasoning; GPT‑4 is strong in broad language understanding; internal models may be fine‑tuned on code.

### Overview of Available Backends

| Backend | Provider | Context Window | Typical Use‑Case | Pricing |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| OpenAI GPT‑4 | OpenAI | 8K / 32K tokens | General coding, complex logic | Pay‑per‑token |
| Anthropic Claude | Anthropic | 100K tokens | Long codebases, deep reasoning | Pay‑per‑token |
| Cursor Internal | Cursor | 4K tokens | Fast suggestions, low‑cost | Included with Cursor subscription |

Choose the backend that aligns with your project size, latency tolerance, and budget.

## Core Mechanics

### How Cursor Communicates with Backends

Cursor acts as a thin wrapper around the underlying REST APIs. When you request a completion, Cursor:

1. **Resolves the model** from the active configuration.
2. **Builds an HTTP request** with the appropriate endpoint (e.g., `https://api.openai.com/v1/chat/completions`).
3. **Attaches authentication** using the API key stored in the environment.
4. **Sends generation parameters** (temperature, max_tokens, …) as JSON payload.
5. **Returns the streamed or batched response** to the editor.

### Adding API Keys Securely

1. **Environment Variables** – Store keys in a `.env` file and load them with a library like `dotenv`. Cursor reads `process.env` automatically.
2. **Cursor Settings UI** – Navigate to **Edit → Preferences → AI** and paste the key into the dedicated field. The key is encrypted at rest.
3. **Secret Managers** – For CI/CD, use services such as AWS Secrets Manager or HashiCorp Vault, and inject the key at runtime.

Never commit API keys to version control. Add `.env` to `.gitignore`.

### Model Configuration Parameters

| Parameter | Description | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| temperature | Controls randomness. Low → deterministic, high → creative. | 0.0 – 1.0 |
| max_tokens | Maximum number of tokens the model can generate. | 1 – model limit |
| top_p | Nucleus sampling threshold. | 0.0 – 1.0 |
| presence_penalty | Discourages repeating topics already mentioned. | -2.0 – 2.0 |
| frequency_penalty | Penalizes repeated token usage. | -2.0 – 2.0 |
| stop | List of tokens that stop generation. | array of strings |

### Switching Models in Cursor

- **UI**: Click the model indicator in the status bar and pick a new backend.
- **Config File**: Edit `~/.cursor/config.json` (or the workspace‑level `.cursor.json`) and set the `model` field.

## Parameter Tuning Guidance

- **Deterministic Code Generation** (e.g., boilerplate): Use temperature 0.0 – 0.2, max_tokens 256.
- **Creative Solutions** (e.g., algorithm brainstorming): Use temperature 0.7 – 0.9, max_tokens 512.
- **Long‑Range Refactoring** (e.g., cross‑file changes): Choose Claude’s 100K context and keep temperature 0.5.
- **Balanced Tasks**: Start with defaults (temperature 0.7, max_tokens 512) and adjust based on output quality.

## Practical Advice

1. **Start Small** – Test new parameters with a handful of prompts before applying them globally.
2. **Monitor Token Usage** – Most providers expose usage endpoints; set alerts when you approach monthly caps.
3. **Leverage System Prompts** – Use a system message to guide behavior (e.g., You are a senior JavaScript developer specializing in performance).
4. **Keep Secrets Out of Code** – Use environment variables or secret managers; never embed keys in scripts.
5. **Version‑Control Configurations** – Store non‑secret settings (model name, default parameters) in version control so the team shares a consistent setup.
6. **Iterate Based on Feedback** – If completions are too generic, raise temperature; if they wander, lower it.

## Code Examples

### Example 1

```python
// Example 1: Using environment variable for API key with OpenAI
require('dotenv').config();
const { OpenAI } = require('openai');

const client = new OpenAI({
  apiKey: process.env.OPENAI_API_KEY
});

async function getCompletion(prompt, model = 'gpt-4', temperature = 0.7, maxTokens = 150) {
  const response = await client.chat.completions.create({
    model: model,
    messages: [{role: 'user', content: prompt}],
    temperature: temperature,
    max_tokens: maxTokens
  });
  return response.choices[0].message.content;
}

// Example usage
getCompletion('Explain the difference between let and const in JavaScript')
  .then(console.log)
  .catch(console.error);
```

### Example 2

```python
# Example 2: Using environment variable for API key with Anthropic Claude
import os
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic(
    api_key=os.environ.get('ANTHROPIC_API_KEY')
)

def get_completion(prompt, model='claude-3-opus-20240229', temperature=0.5, max_tokens=512):
    message = client.messages.create(
        model=model,
        max_tokens=max_tokens,
        temperature=temperature,
        messages=[
            {'role': 'user', 'content': prompt}
        ]
    )
    return message.content[0].text

# Example usage
print(get_completion('What are the benefits of using type hints in Python?'))
```

### Example 3

```python
{
  "model": "cursor-internal-1",
  "parameters": {
    "temperature": 0.8,
    "max_tokens": 512,
    "top_p": 0.95,
    "presence_penalty": 0.0,
    "frequency_penalty": 0.0
  }
}
```



## Common Mistakes

- Storing API keys directly in source code files, leading to accidental exposure in version control.
- Using a high temperature (e.g., 1.0) for deterministic tasks such as code generation, which produces unpredictable output.
- Not setting max_tokens, causing responses to be cut off unexpectedly or exceeding the model's context limit.
- Ignoring the model's context window size, which leads to truncation of long prompts or completions.
- Using the same API key for development and production environments, increasing security risk.
- Failing to handle API errors like rate limits and timeouts, resulting in broken builds or silent failures.


## Best Practices

- Store API keys in environment variables or a secret manager and never commit them to version control.
- Use separate API keys for different environments (dev, staging, prod) to limit exposure.
- Set a reasonable max_tokens based on the expected length of the output to avoid truncation.
- Start with default temperature and adjust based on task; lower values for precise code, higher for creative solutions.
- Monitor token usage and set budget alerts to avoid unexpected costs.
- Version‑control non‑secret configuration files (model name, default parameters) to ensure reproducibility across the team.
code_examples:
# Example 2: Using environment variable for API key with Anthropic Claude
import os
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic(
    api_key=os.environ.get('ANTHROPIC_API_KEY')
)

def get_completion(prompt, model='claude-3-opus-20240229', temperature=0.5, max_tokens=512):
    message = client.messages.create(
        model=model,
        max_tokens=max_tokens,
        temperature=temperature,
        messages=[
            {'role': 'user', 'content': prompt}
        ]
    )
    return message.content[0].text

# Example usage
print(get_completion('What are the benefits of using type hints in Python?'))

Master command palette (Cmd/Ctrl+Shift+P)
Customize keybindings for personal workflow

450.title: First AI‑Assisted Code Snippet
450.description: Hands‑on exercise to generate, accept, and refine a simple code block using AI suggestions.
key_points: Write a natural‑language prompt for a basic function
Accept or modify AI‑generated code
Run the function and verify output
450.detailed_content:
## Concept Explanation

### Why It Matters

AI‑assisted code generation is more than a novelty; it’s a productivity multiplier. By feeding a concise natural‑language description to Cursor AI, you can obtain a ready‑to‑use function in seconds. This workflow helps you:

- **Reduce boilerplate**: Instead of typing repetitive patterns, you get a clean implementation that you can review and refine.
- **Accelerate learning**: When you see a generated solution, you can study idiomatic patterns, naming conventions, and algorithmic approaches you may not have known.
- **Focus on logic**: By offloading syntax‑level work to the AI, you can concentrate on higher‑level design and problem solving.

### Core Mechanics

1. **Write a natural‑language prompt**
   - Describe the function signature, input types, and expected behavior.
   - Mention edge cases if they are important (e.g., “handle an empty list”).
2. **Review the generated code**
   - Cursor AI will display a code block with comments.
   - You may **accept** the suggestion, **modify** parts, or **reject** and refine the prompt.
3. **Run the function and verify output**
   - Execute the code in your environment (REPL, notebook, script).
   - Compare the result against a set of known test cases, including edge cases.

### Hands‑On Example: Factorial in Python

Below is a minimal prompt and the code you might receive. The example shows how to accept the snippet, optionally tweak it, and then test it.

python
# Prompt: Write a Python function that returns the factorial of a non‑negative integer.
def factorial(n):
    """Return n! for non‑negative integer n."""
    if n < 0:
        raise ValueError("Factorial is not defined for negative numbers.")
    if n == 0 or n == 1:
        return 1
    # Use recursion for clarity
    return n * factorial(n - 1)

# Quick test
print(factorial(5))  # Expected: 120


### Hands‑On Example: Sum of Numbers in JavaScript

A similar workflow for JavaScript demonstrates how the language specifics affect the generated code.

javascript
// Prompt: Write a JavaScript function that takes an array of numbers and returns their sum.
function sumArray(arr) {
    // Guard against non‑array input
    if (!Array.isArray(arr)) {
        throw new TypeError("Input must be an array.");
    }
    // Use reduce to accumulate values
    return arr.reduce((acc, val) => acc + (typeof val === 'number' ? val : 0), 0);
}

// Test cases
console.log(sumArray([1, 2, 3]));      // Expected: 6
console.log(sumArray([]));            // Expected: 0
console.log(sumArray([4, 'a', 2]));    // Expected: 6 (non‑numbers are ignored)


### Why Iteration Matters

AI models generate plausible code, but they can also introduce subtle bugs or ignore edge cases. By iterating—changing the prompt, adding constraints, or editing the generated snippet—you achieve a solution that truly fits your project’s requirements.

### Tips for Effective Prompts

- **Be explicit about types**: e.g., “parameter `n` is an integer”.
- **State expected output**: e.g., “return the factorial as an integer”.
- **Mention constraints**: e.g., “do not use built‑in factorial functions”.
- **Include test scenarios**: “the function should return 0 for an empty array”.

### Summary

- Write a clear, concise prompt.
- Review, accept, or modify the AI suggestion.
- Run the code and validate with a suite of test cases.
- Iterate to refine both the prompt and the implementation.

By mastering this first AI‑assisted snippet workflow, you lay the foundation for more complex, multi‑step AI‑augmented development sessions.

## Code Examples

### Example 1

```python
# Python: Compute factorial
# Prompt: Write a Python function that returns the factorial of a non‑negative integer.
def factorial(n):
    """Return n! for non‑negative integer n."""
    if n < 0:
        raise ValueError("Factorial is not defined for negative numbers.")
    if n == 0 or n == 1:
        return 1
    # Recursive case
    return n * factorial(n - 1)

# Simple test
print("5! =", factorial(5))  # Expected: 120
```

### Example 2

```python
# JavaScript: Sum an array of numbers
// Prompt: Write a JavaScript function that takes an array of numbers and returns their sum.
function sumArray(arr) {
    if (!Array.isArray(arr)) {
        throw new TypeError('Input must be an array.');
    }
    // Use reduce, ignoring non‑numeric values
    return arr.reduce((sum, val) => sum + (typeof val === 'number' ? val : 0), 0);
}

// Test cases
console.log(sumArray([1, 2, 3]));      // Expected: 6
console.log(sumArray([]));            // Expected: 0
console.log(sumArray([4, 'a', 2]));   // Expected: 6
```



## Common Mistakes

- Writing vague prompts that do not specify input types or expected behavior, leading to unexpected function signatures.
- Accepting AI‑generated code without reviewing it for correctness, missing edge‑case handling or off‑by‑one errors.
- Not testing the snippet with a variety of inputs, especially boundary conditions like empty collections or negative numbers.
- Assuming the AI always follows language‑specific best practices, which can result in deprecated APIs or non‑idiomatic style.
- Skipping documentation or comments, making future maintenance harder when the original intent is unclear.


## Best Practices

- Craft prompts that are concise yet complete: state the function name (if desired), input types, output type, and notable constraints.
- Always review the generated code line‑by‑line before integrating it; treat it as a draft, not a final product.
- Add your own comments and docstrings to clarify purpose, parameters, and return values, reinforcing your understanding.
- Test the snippet with a suite of test cases, including normal, edge, and error‑case inputs, and verify the output matches expectations.
- Use version control to commit each iteration of the prompt and resulting code, allowing you to roll back if later changes introduce bugs.
- Iterate quickly: if the first suggestion is not ideal, refine the prompt and request a new generation rather than patching extensively.

451.title: Rules & Skills: Permanent AI Instructions
451.description: How to write, version‑control, and apply project‑wide instructions that the AI follows in every session.
key_points: Define coding standards, naming conventions, and security guidelines
Organize Rules files per module or feature
Debug and iterate on instruction quality

452.title: Composer for Multi‑File Editing
452.description: Leverage Cursor’s Composer to generate or refactor several files in a single request, maintaining consistency across the codebase.
key_points: Draft composite prompts that specify multiple file targets
Review generated diffs before committing changes
Apply bulk refactors (e.g., renaming, import cleanup)
452.detailed_content:
# Composer for Multi-File Editing

## Concept Overview

Cursor's Composer is a powerful feature designed to handle multi-file generation and refactoring in a single request. Unlike traditional single-file editing where you work with one file at a time, Composer enables you to target multiple files simultaneously while maintaining structural consistency and code coherence across your entire codebase.

### Why It Matters

Modern applications rarely exist in isolation. A typical feature might require changes to:

- A component file
- Its corresponding styles
- Test files
- Type definitions
- State management logic

Without Composer, you'd need to make these changes sequentially, risking inconsistency and requiring you to manually track which files you've already modified. Composer solves this by treating your codebase as a cohesive unit rather than a collection of disconnected files.

## Core Mechanics

### How Composer Processes Multi-File Requests

1. **Composite Prompt Structure**: You construct a prompt that explicitly identifies target files and describes the desired changes for each.

2. **Context Awareness**: Composer analyzes relationships between files—imports, dependencies, and shared types—to ensure changes align with existing architecture.

3. **Atomic Generation**: All specified files are generated or modified in a single pass, ensuring consistency in naming conventions, coding style, and implementation patterns.

4. **Diff Presentation**: Changes appear as a unified diff view, allowing you to review all modifications before applying them.

### Key Capabilities

| Capability | Description |
|------------|-------------|
| Batch Generation | Create multiple related files from a single prompt |
| Bulk Refactoring | Rename symbols or restructure code across multiple files |
| Import Management | Automatically update import statements when files move |
| Consistency Enforcement | Apply uniform patterns across all targeted files |

## Practical Workflow

### Drafting Composite Prompts

When writing multi-file prompts, structure matters. Include these elements:

- **Explicit file paths**: Specify exact locations for each file
- **Clear purpose**: Describe what each file should contain
- **Relationships**: Explain how files interact with each other
- **Constraints**: Mention coding standards or patterns to follow

### Review Process

1. Generate the changes
2. Review the diff view thoroughly
3. Check each file individually for accuracy
4. Verify imports and exports are correct
5. Confirm changes align with your codebase conventions
6. Apply only if satisfied

## Common Use Cases

### Feature Creation

Create a complete feature module including:

- Main component
- Sub-components
- Utility functions
- Type definitions
- Associated tests

### Architectural Changes

Refactor codebases by:

- Renaming variables across multiple files
- Extracting shared logic into utility modules
- Restructuring folder hierarchies
- Updating deprecated patterns

### Import Cleanup

Remove unused imports, organize import statements, or update paths when restructuring files.

## Important Considerations

While Composer is powerful, it requires careful usage. Always verify generated code matches your expectations, especially when working with critical files or making architectural changes.

## Best Practices Summary

- Always review diffs before committing
- Use explicit file paths in prompts
- Keep prompts focused on specific features
- Maintain regular version control check-ins


## Code Examples

### Example 1

```python
// Example 1: Multi-file feature generation prompt
// Use this pattern when you need to create multiple related files
// Paste this into Composer to generate a complete feature module

/*
Create a user authentication module with the following structure:

1. src/auth/context/AuthContext.tsx
   - React Context with user state management
   - Include login(), logout(), and useAuth() hook
   - Handle loading and error states

2. src/auth/types/index.ts
   - Export User interface with id, email, name, role
   - Export AuthState interface with user, isAuthenticated, isLoading
   - Export AuthContextType interface

3. src/auth/hooks/useAuth.ts
   - Custom hook that wraps useContext(AuthContext)
   - Provide TypeScript types for the return value
   - Include error handling for missing provider

4. src/auth/services/authService.ts
   - Async functions: loginUser(), logoutUser(), fetchCurrentUser()
   - Mock API delay of 500ms for simulation
   - Return proper success/error responses

5. src/auth/__tests__/AuthContext.test.tsx
   - Test provider rendering
   - Test login/logout functionality
   - Test auth state transitions
   - Use @testing-library/react

Follow these conventions:
- Use TypeScript strict mode
- No console.log statements
- Include JSDoc comments for public functions
- Use named exports only
*/
```

### Example 2

```python
// Example 2: Bulk refactoring prompt
// Use this pattern when you need to rename or restructure code across files

/*
Perform the following refactoring across the entire codebase to rename legacy patterns to modern patterns:

File targets:
1. All .ts and .tsx files in src/components/
2. All .ts files in src/utils/
3. src/types/index.ts

Changes to apply:

A. Rename pattern (API-first to promise-based):
   - `getDataUsingCallback(callback)` → `getData()` returning Promise
   - Update all call sites to use async/await
   - Remove callback parameters from function signatures

B. Rename utility functions:
   - `formatDateString(date: Date)` → `formatDate(date: Date)`
   - `calculateSumOfArray(arr: number[])` → `sum(numbers: number[])`
   - `createUUID()` → `generateId()`

C. Update import statements:
   - Remove imports from 'utils/helpers' (now in 'utils/')
   - Add 'type' keyword to type-only imports

D. Code style fixes:
   - Convert all function declarations to arrow functions
   - Replace var with const where appropriate
   - Add explicit return types to all exported functions

Before generating, analyze the dependency graph to ensure imports are updated correctly.
After generating, verify all exports/imports remain consistent.
*/
```

### Example 3

```python
// Example 3: Import cleanup and organization prompt
// Use this pattern for bulk import management

/*
Clean up imports across the following files:

Target files:
- src/features/user/*.tsx
- src/features/user/*.ts
- src/features/user/components/*.tsx

Cleanup tasks:

1. Remove unused imports:
   - Scan each file for imports that are not referenced
   - Remove only truly unused imports, not conditional ones

2. Organize import order:
   - React and Next.js imports first (external frameworks)
   - Third-party libraries (alphabetical)
   - Internal imports (shorter paths first)
   - Type imports (use 'import type' syntax)

3. Simplify import paths:
   - Replace '../components/Button' with '@/components/Button' if using path aliases
   - Consolidate multiple imports from same package into single line

4. Convert default imports to named where applicable:
   - Look for 'import React from "react"' → 'import { useState } from "react"'
   - Only convert if fewer than 3 named imports from package

5. Add missing type imports:
   - Convert 'import { User }' to 'import type { User }' where User is only used as a type

Provide a summary of all changes made for review.
*/
```



## Common Mistakes

- Not reviewing generated diffs thoroughly before applying changes, which can introduce subtle bugs or inconsistencies across files
- Writing vague prompts that don't specify exact file paths, causing Composer to generate files in unexpected locations or with inconsistent naming
- Overloading a single prompt with too many files or too many types of changes, leading to partial or incorrect implementations
- Forgetting to check inter-file dependencies like imports, exports, and shared types after bulk generation
- Assuming changes are atomic and complete without verifying that all referenced files have been updated
- Not committing to version control before applying bulk changes, making rollback difficult if issues are discovered later


## Best Practices

- Always draft prompts that explicitly list each target file with its path and intended purpose, making your intent unambiguous
- Review generated changes incrementally—apply and test changes in small batches rather than committing all files at once
- Use consistent naming conventions and code style guidelines in your prompts to ensure uniformity across generated files
- Maintain regular version control commits so you can easily revert if bulk changes introduce unexpected issues
- Verify inter-file consistency by checking that imports, exports, and shared types align correctly after generation
- Split complex multi-file requests into smaller, focused prompts that handle single concerns (e.g., separate prompts for types, components, and utilities)
- Test generated code in isolation and then verify integration works correctly before considering the task complete
code_examples:

// Use this pattern when you need to create multiple related files
// Paste this into Composer to generate a complete feature module

/*
Create a user authentication module with the following structure:

1. src/auth/context/AuthContext.tsx
   - React Context with user state management
   - Include login(), logout(), and useAuth() hook
   - Handle loading and error states

2. src/auth/types/index.ts

// Example 2: Bulk refactoring prompt

/*

3. src/types/index.ts

Changes to apply:

A. Rename pattern (API-first to promise-based):
   - `getDataUsingCallback(callback)` → `getData()` returning Promise
   - Update all call sites to use async/await

B. Rename utility functions:
   - `formatDateString(date: Date)` → `formatDate(date: Date)`
   - `calculateSumOfArray(arr: number[])` → `sum(numbers: number[])`

2. Organize import order:

4. Convert default imports to named where applicable:
   - Look for 'import React from "react"' → 'import { useState } from "react"'
   - Only convert if fewer than 3 named imports from package

5. Add missing type imports:
   - Convert 'import { User }' to 'import type { User }' where User is only used as a type

Provide a summary of all changes made for review.
*/

453.title: Prompt Engineering & Context Management
453.description: Techniques for structuring prompts, injecting relevant context, and steering AI behavior for precise code generation.
key_points: Break complex tasks into sequential, incremental prompts
Embed code snippets, comments, and API docs as context
Use explicit constraints to limit scope and enforce style
453.detailed_content:
## Prompt Engineering & Context Management in Cursor AI

### Concept Explanation: Why This Matters

Prompt engineering is the art of communicating your intent to an AI system in ways that consistently produce the desired output. In the context of integrated programming workflows with Cursor AI, effective prompt engineering isn't just about getting code to work—it's about creating a collaborative environment where the AI understands not just *what* you want, but *how* you want it, *why* you need it that way, and *where* it fits into your broader project architecture.

When you master prompt engineering and context management, you transform Cursor AI from a simple autocomplete tool into a true pair programming partner. The difference between a junior developer who struggles with AI tools and a senior developer who integrates them seamlessly often comes down to their ability to articulate requirements, provide appropriate context, and guide the AI's reasoning process through structured communication.

Context management is equally critical because AI systems have limited working memory. The model processes text sequentially, and information presented earlier in a conversation has a outsized influence on subsequent outputs. Without proper context management, you might find the AI contradicting itself, ignoring your previous instructions, or generating code that doesn't integrate with your existing codebase.

### Core Mechanics: How It Works

#### The Sequential Prompting Strategy

Complex programming tasks rarely succeed when attempted in a single prompt. When you ask Cursor AI to build an entire feature from scratch, the model must juggle architecture decisions, implementation details, error handling, testing considerations, and style consistency—all within a single response. This cognitive overload leads to suboptimal results.

The sequential prompting strategy addresses this by breaking tasks into digestible pieces. Instead of asking "build a user authentication system," you decompose the request into logical phases: first establish the data models, then implement the core authentication logic, add security middleware, create the API endpoints, and finally write tests. Each prompt builds upon the previous work, creating a coherent development trajectory.

This approach offers several advantages. First, it allows you to catch and correct errors early, before they compound. Second, it gives you granular control over each component's implementation. Third, it reduces the AI's working memory burden, allowing it to focus its attention on the specific task at hand. Fourth, it creates natural checkpoints where you can evaluate progress and adjust direction.

#### Context Injection Techniques

Context injection involves providing Cursor AI with relevant information that shapes its responses. Effective context injection goes beyond simply pasting your entire codebase into the prompt—it's about selecting and presenting information strategically.

**Code Snippets as Context**: When you want the AI to follow existing patterns, provide concrete examples from your codebase. Instead of describing "our error handling style," show actual error handling code from your project. The model can then extract patterns and apply them consistently.

**Documentation References**: Including relevant documentation—whether from language standard libraries, framework APIs, or your own project documentation—helps the AI generate code that uses APIs correctly and follows established conventions.

**Comments and Annotations**: Adding explanatory comments within code snippets helps the AI understand not just what the code does, but why it was written that way. This prevents well-intentioned "improvements" that might break implicit assumptions.

**Project Structure Information**: Providing a brief overview of your project structure helps the AI place new code in the right locations and understand import/export relationships.

#### Constraint-Based Steering

Explicit constraints guide AI behavior by narrowing the solution space. Without constraints, the AI may generate code that's technically correct but doesn't fit your requirements—perhaps using a different coding style, targeting a different Node.js version, or solving a slightly different problem than intended.

Constraints can be positive (requirements the code must satisfy) or negative (restrictions on what the code can do). Positive constraints include performance requirements, compatibility targets, and feature specifications. Negative constraints include avoiding certain patterns, libraries, or approaches that have proven problematic in your context.

Effective constraints are specific and measurable. "Make the function fast" is less useful than "Ensure the function processes 10,000 records in under 100ms on the target hardware." The more precisely you can specify your requirements, the more reliably the AI can meet them.

### Practical Implementation

The three key techniques—sequential prompting, context injection, and constraint-based steering—work best when applied together. When crafting a prompt for Cursor AI, consider each element:

1. **Clear objective**: What specific task do you want accomplished in this prompt?
2. **Relevant context**: What information does the AI need to understand your situation?
3. **Explicit constraints**: What requirements or restrictions apply to this task?
4. **Feedback mechanism**: How will you evaluate whether the output meets your needs?

### Common Pitfalls and Gotchas

**Context Overload**: Providing too much context dilutes the important information. The AI weights information by recency and prominence, so burying critical requirements in a sea of background noise often results in those requirements being ignored. Include only the context directly relevant to the current task.

**Implicit Assumptions**: The AI cannot read your mind. Assumptions you consider obvious may be completely invisible to the model. When something matters, state it explicitly. If you need the code to work offline, say so. If alphabetical ordering is important, mention it.

**Overly Broad Prompts**: Asking for "the best" or "optimal" solution invites the AI to make trade-offs based on its training data's priorities, which may not align with yours. Define what "best" means in your specific context.

**Inconsistent Terminology**: Using different terms for the same concept confuses the AI and leads to inconsistent code. Establish terminology early and maintain it throughout the conversation.

**Ignoring Error Messages**: When Cursor AI generates code that produces errors, simply complaining to the AI isn't enough. Analyze the error, provide the error message along with relevant context about what you were trying to accomplish, and ask for a specific fix.

### Practical Advice for Intermediate Users

**Start with Exploration**: Before diving into implementation, use a prompt to understand the problem space. Ask Cursor AI to explain concepts, suggest approaches, or outline architecture. This exploration phase often reveals considerations you'd otherwise miss.

**Iterate Incrementally**: Build your solution piece by piece. Get each component working correctly before moving to the next. This creates a solid foundation and makes debugging easier when issues arise.

**Maintain a Prompt Journal**: Keep notes on which prompts work well for your projects. Over time, you'll develop a library of effective prompting patterns specific to your tech stack and coding style.

**Use System Messages Wisely**: If your project has consistent requirements—coding standards, architecture patterns, testing requirements—establish these in early interactions and reference them when needed. You can create reference prompts that define these standards for reuse.

**Validate Before Proceeding**: Don't assume the AI's output is correct. Test it, review it, and validate it against your requirements before building upon it. Catching errors early saves significant time.

**Embrace Collaboration**: Think of Cursor AI as a junior colleague who is extremely knowledgeable but needs clear direction. Your role is to provide context, clarify requirements, and review work. The quality of your guidance directly determines the quality of the output.

## Code Examples

### Example 1

```python
python
# Example 1: Sequential Prompting for Building a REST API Endpoint
# This demonstrates breaking a complex task into incremental prompts

# PROMPT 1: Define the data model and schema
"""
I'm building a Python FastAPI application with SQLAlchemy. Create a User model 
with the following fields:
- id: UUID primary key
- email: unique string, required
- hashed_password: string, required
- created_at: datetime, auto-set on creation
- is_active: boolean, default True
- role: string enum ['admin', 'user', 'guest']

Include proper type hints and SQLAlchemy column definitions.
"""

# After receiving the model, proceed to PROMPT 2
# PROMPT 2: Add repository layer for database operations
"""
Based on the User model from our previous conversation, create a UserRepository 
class with async methods for:
- get_by_id(user_id: UUID) -> Optional[User]
- get_by_email(email: str) -> Optional[User]
- create(user_data: UserCreate) -> User
- update(user_id: UUID, update_data: UserUpdate) -> Optional[User]

Use SQLAlchemy async session patterns. Include proper error handling and logging.
"""

# PROMPT 3: Create the API endpoint with constraints
"""
Create a POST /users endpoint that:
- Accepts email and password in the request body
- Validates email format and password strength (min 8 chars, 1 uppercase, 1 number)
- Uses the UserRepository to create the user
- Returns 201 on success with the created user (excluding password)
- Returns 409 if email already exists
- Uses dependency injection for the repository
- Follows our project's error handling pattern with custom HTTPExceptions

Keep the response model minimal: only include id, email, created_at, and role.
"""

# RESULT: Each step builds on the previous, creating a coherent API endpoint
# with proper separation of concerns and consistent patterns throughout.

```

### Example 2

```python
python
# Example 2: Context Injection with Code Snippets and Constraints
# This demonstrates how to guide AI behavior through explicit context

# INITIAL PROMPT WITH ESTABLISHED CONTEXT
"""
I'm adding pagination to our existing API. Here's our current pagination utility 
that you should follow as a pattern:

python
class Paginator:
    def __init__(self, page: int, per_page: int, total: int):
        self.page = page
        self.per_page = per_page
        self.total = total
        self.pages = (total + per_page - 1) // per_page
    
    @property
    def has_prev(self) -> bool:
        return self.page > 1
    
    @property
    def has_next(self) -> bool:
        return self.page < self.pages
    
    @property
    def offset(self) -> int:
        return (self.page - 1) * self.per_page
    
    def to_dict(self) -> dict:
        return {
            'page': self.page,
            'per_page': self.per_page,
            'total': self.total,
            'pages': self.pages,
            'has_prev': self.has_prev,
            'has_next': self.has_next
        }


Create a paginated response wrapper for our list endpoints with these constraints:
1. MUST follow the Paginator class pattern exactly (same method names, properties)
2. The response wrapper should include 'items' (list) and 'pagination' (Paginator)
3. Add a classmethod from_count(total: int) that accepts just the total count
4. Include proper generic typing for the items list
5. Do NOT add any additional fields beyond 'items' and 'pagination'
6. Follow the same docstring style used in the example

This approach ensures consistency with existing code while adding new functionality.
"""

# The resulting code will integrate seamlessly with existing pagination utilities
# because we provided the exact pattern to follow and explicit constraints.

# Example of how to then USE the paginated response:
def get_users(page: int = 1, per_page: int = 20) -> PaginatedResponse[UserSchema]:
    """Example endpoint using the paginated response wrapper."""
    users = user_repository.get_users(offset=(page-1)*per_page, limit=per_page)
    total = user_repository.count_users()
    
    return PaginatedResponse[
        UserSchema
    ].from_count(items=users, page=page, per_page=per_page, total=total)

```



## Common Mistakes

- Providing too much context at once, burying critical requirements in verbose background information that the AI may not fully process
- Assuming the AI remembers previous conversation turns perfectly—each new prompt should include necessary context rather than relying on implicit memory
- Using vague constraints like 'make it good' or 'follow best practices' instead of specific, measurable requirements that the AI can actually satisfy
- Pasting entire files when only specific snippets are relevant, causing the AI to focus on irrelevant details and miss the actual point
- Not specifying the tech stack, version requirements, or library constraints, leading to code that uses unavailable features or incompatible patterns
- Asking multiple unrelated questions in a single prompt, which splits the AI's attention and results in partial or unfocused answers
- Neglecting to mention existing code patterns or style conventions, resulting in new code that doesn't match the project's established approach
- Assuming the AI will catch errors or edge cases without explicit instruction to do so—the AI often takes the most direct path unless told otherwise


## Best Practices

- Structure complex tasks into a series of focused, sequential prompts where each builds upon the previous, allowing for iterative refinement and early error detection
- Always provide concrete code examples from your project when you want the AI to follow existing patterns—this is far more effective than describing patterns in words
- Include explicit constraints using keywords like 'MUST', 'MUST NOT', 'REQUIRED', and 'DO NOT' to clearly delineate boundaries on acceptable solutions
- Reference specific documentation, API versions, or configuration details relevant to the task to ensure the AI generates compatible code
- After receiving output, verify it meets your requirements before proceeding to the next step—this prevents compounding errors throughout a project
- Maintain consistent terminology throughout your conversation and with your codebase to avoid confusion between similar concepts
- When encountering errors, provide the full error message along with context about what you were attempting, asking for specific fixes rather than general help
- Use the AI's strength in pattern matching by showing examples of both what you want AND what you want to avoid, providing contrast that clarifies your intent
